{{Expand Japanese|平群石床神社}}

{{Infobox religious building
| name = Hegurinoiwatoko Shrine
| native_name = 平群石床神社
| mapframe = yes

{{nihongo|'''Hegurinoiwatoko Shrine'''|平群石床神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. It is located in [[Yamato Province]].

== instance of (P31) ==

* {{ill|Shinto shrine|lt=Shinto shrine|ar|جينجا|arz|ضريح شنتو|bcl|Shinto shrine|ca|Jinja|cs|Šintoistická svatyně|da|Shinto-tempel|de|Shintō-Schrein|el|Σιντοϊστικό Ιερό|en|Shinto shrine|eo|Ŝintoa sanktejo|es|Jinja|eu|Jinja|fa|معبد شینتویی|fi|Šintolainen pyhäkkö|fr|Sanctuaire shinto|hi|शिंतो मंदिर|hr|Šintoistički hram|hu|Sintó szentély|id|Jinja|it|Jinja|ja|神社|ka|ძინძია|ko|신사 (신토)|lb|Shintō-Schräin|mr|शिंतो मंदिर|nl|Jinja (heiligdom)|no|Jinja|or|ଜିଞ୍ଜା|pl|Jinja|pt|Templo xintoísta|ru|Дзиндзя|sc|Jinja|simple|Shinto shrine|sl|Šintoistično svetišče|sr|Шинтоистички храм|sv|Jinja|th|ศาลเจ้าชินโต|tr|Şinto tapınağı|uk|Святилище (синто)|vi|Thần xã|wuu|神社|zh_yue|神社|zh|神社|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}}

== Japanese content ==
{{Shrine
| Name       = Heguri Iwatoko Shrine
| Image      = {{Image requested|cat=Ikoma District}}
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|34|37|16|N|135|41|42|E}}}}
| Location   = {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|qid=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Ikoma District|Q971003|qid=Q971003|1=Ikoma District, Nara}}{{ill|lt=Heguri Town|Q1346790|qid=Q1346790|1=Heguri, Nara}} 734 Koshizuka
| ISO        = JP-29
| Latitude   = 34 | LatitudeMin = 37 | LatitudeSec = 16
| Longitude  = 135 | LongitudeMin = 41 | LongitudeSec = 42
| Enshrined deity = Kenjin Iwatoko Wake no Mikoto (or {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nigihayahi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}})
| Map        = Japan Nara
}}
[[File:Heguri Iwatoko Shrine.jpg|thumbnail|Former shrine site of Heguri Iwatoko Shrine]]
**Heguri Iwatoko Shrine** (へぐりのいわとこじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} enshrined in Koshizuka, {{ill|lt=Heguri Town|Q1346790|qid=Q1346790|1=Heguri, Nara}}, {{ill|lt=Ikoma District|Q971003|qid=Q971003|1=Ikoma District, Nara}}, {{ill|lt=Nara Prefecture|Q131287|qid=Q131287|1=Nara Prefecture}}.

It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} listed as a grand shrine in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|qid=Q1342448|1=Engishiki}} {{ill|lt=Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and it was designated to receive ritual offerings at the imperial court’s {{ill|lt=Tsukinami-sai|Q11516161|qid=Q11516161|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri}} and {{ill|lt=Niiname-sai|Q11501518|qid=Q11501518|1=Niiname-no-Matsuri}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Village Shrine|Q134917284|qid=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}.

=== Overview ===
Below the settlement of Koshizuka in Heguri Town, a massive rock approximately 6 meters high and 10 meters wide remains on a cliff. Since ancient times, this {{ill|lt=iwakura|Q11587880|qid=Q11587880|1=Iwakura rock}} has been worshipped as the sacred body of the deity. There are only several stone steps, a single stone lantern, and a wooden torii gate; originally, this shrine had neither a main sanctuary nor a worship hall.

The giant rock resembles {{ill|lt=female genitalia|Q844342|qid=Q844342|1=Female reproductive system}} and is therefore also called an *in-seki* (yin stone).<ref>{{Cite web|Japanese|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/陰陽石-208553 |title=陰陽石 |access-date=2022-09-26}}</ref>

The enshrined deity is Kenjin Iwatoko Wake no Mikoto, though there is also a theory identifying the deity as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Nigihayahi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}.<ref>In the *Nihon Sōkoku Fudoki*, the deity is listed as {{ill|lt=Nigihayahi no Mikoto|Q10949054|qid=Q10949054|1=Nigihayahi}}.</ref>

The shrine is listed among those promoted in rank on the 27th day of the first month of the first year of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|1=Jōgan}} during the reign of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seiwa|Q349243|qid=Q349243|1=Emperor Seiwa}}, when it was granted the rank of “Heguri Iwatoko Shrine, Junior Fifth Rank, Upper.”<ref>{{Cite book|Japanese|editor=Heguri Town History Editorial Committee|title=History of Heguri Town|date=1976-04|publisher=Heguri Town Office|ncid=BN04492478|pages=667–670}}</ref>

During the {{ill|lt=Taishō period|Q329174|qid=Q329174|1=Taishō era}}, a main hall and worship hall were constructed at the site of an auxiliary shrine outside the precincts (the Susanoo Shrine that had been merged in 1911), while the former sacred site was left as it was. This location is about a four-minute walk from the original shrine site.

The auxiliary shrines to the left and right are said to enshrine {{ill|lt=Futodama no Mikoto|Q11059539|qid=Q11059539|1=Futodama}} (left when facing the shrine) and {{ill|lt=Susanoo no Mikoto|Q272993|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} (right when facing the shrine). There is also a sacred object known as a {{ill|lt=Kōgoishi|Q6455119|qid=Q6455119|1=Kōgoishi}}.

On the mountain opposite stands {{ill|lt=Funayama Shrine|Q119929665|qid=Q119929665|1=Funayama Shrine}}, where a *yō-seki* (yang stone) is worshipped. The two shrines are sometimes referred to as a pair: “Iwatoko Shrine of the West and Funayama Shrine of the East.”

Within the precincts is Shōkachi Shrine, whose enshrined deity is {{ill|lt=Ame-no-Oshihomimi no Mikoto|Q462555|qid=Q462555|1=Ame-no-oshihomimi}}, regarded as the {{ill|lt=ujigami|Q1392097|qid=Q1392097|1=Ubusunagami}} and known as the “god of righteous victory.”<ref>Shrine notice board</ref> However, it was originally a subsidiary shrine of the {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} Shrine that had been merged in ancient times.<ref>An inscription on the stone lantern behind the worship hall reads: “Everlasting Lantern, Koshizuka Daimyōjin, erected in the seventh month of the thirteenth year of {{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|qid=Q1327119|1=Bunsei}}, year of the Tiger.”</ref> During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}, the shrine attracted worshippers from {{ill|lt=Gion|Q926312|qid=Q926312|1=Gion}} in Kyoto, as it was believed—based on its name—to be effective against {{ill|lt=women’s illnesses|UNKNOWN|lt=female diseases}} and {{ill|lt=venereal diseases|Q11491613|qid=Q11491613|1=venereal diseases}}. It is said that teahouses even lined the approach due to the crowds. A custom of {{ill|lt=making votive prayers|Q11665156|qid=Q11665156|1=gankake}} continues today: twelve earthen dumplings are made and offered at a small shelter at the bottom of the shrine steps when making a wish, and if the wish is fulfilled, twelve rice dumplings are offered in thanks.

On the upper-left hill of Shōkachi Shrine stands {{ill|lt=Nanasha Shrine|Q11353657|qid=Q11353657|1=Nanasha Shrine}}, which enshrines the Seven-Shrine Daimyōjin.<ref>In Meiji-period shrine records, the enshrined deity is Ame-no-Koyane no Mikoto.</ref>

Additionally, on the right side of the approach to the shrine is the sacred spring (*goshinsui*) associated with Shōkachi Shrine.
